description: Bar charts comparing yield stresses measured at 650 °C in compression (dark gray bars) and tension (light gray bars) for two series of iron aluminide alloys: Fe–15Al-based alloys (subfigure a) and Fe–26Al-based alloys (subfigure b). Subfigure (a) presents data for binary Fe-15Al and quaternary alloys with 2 at.% Ti, V, Nb, or Ta additions plus 1 at.% C (Fe-15Al-2Ti-1C, Fe-15Al-2V-1C, Fe-15Al-2Nb-1C, Fe-15Al-2Ta-1C), showing yield stresses ranging from approximately 80–120 MPa for the binary alloy to 330–430 MPa for Nb/Ta-containing alloys in tension, with consistently higher values in compression. Subfigure (b) displays Fe-26Al binary alloy and alloys with varying Ti (2 or 4 at.%), V (2 at.%), Nb (2 or 4 at.%), and Ta (2 at.%), with carbon additions, showing yield stresses from approximately 95–155 MPa for the binary alloy up to 210–330 MPa for higher Nb-content alloys. The data visually demonstrates pronounced tension–compression asymmetry across all compositions, with compression yield stresses typically exceeding tensile values by 30–100 MPa. The charts reveal that Nb and Ta additions provide the most significant strengthening effects at 650 °C compared to Ti or V additions, and that Fe-26Al-based alloys generally exhibit higher absolute yield strengths than their Fe-15Al counterparts.
